questions.text: Who is most commonly associated with the perpetration of fraudulent financial reports?
questions.tag: Perpetrator Profile
options: A. Operational-level employees with lower hierarchical rank
B. Independent external auditors
C. Senior management and organizational executives
D. External customers and users of financial statements
questions.justification_correct: Senior management possesses the authority, access, and capability to manipulate accounting policies and suppress control mechanisms, making them the most frequent perpetrator profile in this category. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Operational-level employees have restricted access; external auditors have a verification role; external customers lack authority over financial statements.

questions.text: Which financial statement is most frequently targeted for manipulation in accounting fraud schemes?
questions.tag: Financial Statements
options: A. Statement of Cash Flows
B. Balance Sheet
C. Income Statement
D. Statement of Changes in Equity
questions.justification_correct: The Income Statement is the primary target for manipulation as it reports profits and operating results, directly linked to market expectations and performance targets. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: The other statements may also be manipulated, but the Income Statement concentrates the highest incidence of fraud due to its direct connection with performance indicators.

questions.text: Which accounting estimate presents greater vulnerability to management manipulation?
questions.tag: Accounting Estimates
options: A. Cash and cash equivalents balance
B. Depreciation rate and useful life
C. Balance of accounts payable to suppliers
D. Value of loans and bank financing
questions.justification_correct: Depreciation depends on judgments regarding useful life and residual value, offering room for manipulation that directly impacts reported earnings. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Cash, accounts payable, and bank loans are objective values with less dependence on subjective estimates.

questions.text: Which financial ratio can signal the occurrence of artificially inflated revenues?
questions.tag: Ratio Analysis
options: A. Current liquidity ratio
B. Inventory turnover
C. Accounts receivable turnover
D. Debt-to-equity ratio
questions.justification_correct: A slowdown in accounts receivable turnover may indicate fictitious or unrealized sales, where receivables grow without correspondence to actual business activity. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Current liquidity, inventory turnover, and debt ratios are relevant indicators, but receivables turnover has a more direct connection to fraud detection.

questions.text: Which characteristic of the business environment elevates the risk of fraud in financial reporting?
questions.tag: Risk Factors
options: A. Stable and predictable demand for the organization's products
B. A highly structured and supervised regulatory environment
C. Pressure for accelerated growth and ambitious performance targets
D. Adoption of conservative accounting policies
questions.justification_correct: Pressure for high growth creates incentives for results manipulation, especially when managers have variable compensation tied to financial performance. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Stable demand, structured regulation, and conservative accounting are factors that reduce the risk of fraud.

questions.text: Which document represents the most useful source in detecting revenue manipulation?
questions.tag: Evidence Sources
options: A. Sales contracts and agreements
B. Payroll records
C. Income tax returns
D. Fixed asset registers
questions.justification_correct: Sales contracts establish the terms, conditions, and timing of revenue recognition, enabling comparison of the accounting entry with actual contractual obligations. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Payroll, tax returns, and asset records are relevant sources for other types of analysis, but contracts are priority in revenue analysis.

questions.text: Which organizational factor is most effective in limiting fraudulent financial reporting?
questions.tag: Prevention
options: A. External competitive market forces
B. Consolidated and disseminated ethical culture
C. Sustained high profitability
D. Accelerated business growth
questions.justification_correct: A strong ethical culture, with appropriate tone at the top, discourages intentional misrepresentation and creates an organizational environment resistant to fraud. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Market forces, profitability, and growth are external or financial factors that do not, in themselves, prevent accounting fraud.

questions.text: Which mechanism is frequently utilized to conceal fraud in financial statements?
questions.tag: Concealment
options: A. Regular write-offs of obsolete assets
B. Expansion and transparency in financial disclosures
C. Related party transactions for earnings displacement
D. Detailed cash flow reports
questions.justification_correct: Related party transactions allow the displacement of profits, concealment of losses, or creation of fictitious revenue through entities controlled by the perpetrator. (CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024)
questions.justification_wrong: Regular write-offs, transparent disclosures, and detailed reports are governance practices that reduce, rather than increase, opportunities for concealment.

questions.text: Which preventive control is most effective in combating financial statement fraud?
questions.tag: Preventive Controls
options: A. Physical security of facilities and tangible assets
B. Segregation of duties in accounting processes
C. Ethical leadership and independent board oversight
D. Periodic physical inventory counts
questions.justification_correct: The tone at the top—ethical leadership combined with independent oversight—is the most effective control against financial statement fraud, as it directly addresses incentives and organizational culture. (CFE Fraud Examiner)
questions.justification_wrong: Physical security and inventory counts are controls aimed at asset misappropriation; segregation of duties is relevant but less effective than ethical leadership and oversight.

questions.text: What is the most effective and structured response when financial statement fraud is suspected?
questions.tag: Fraud Response
options: A. Publicly accuse management to pressure them into confession
B. Ignore discrepancies of apparent minor materiality
C. Conduct a structured investigation with professional methodology
D. Immediately reissue financial statements without prior investigation
questions.justification_correct: A structured investigation conducted with professional methodology and based on evidence is the appropriate response that preserves the integrity of the process and the rights of the investigated parties. (CFE Fraud Examiner)
questions.justification_wrong: Public accusations, ignoring discrepancies, and reissuing statements without investigation are inappropriate responses that may aggravate damages and expose the organization to additional liability.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a manufacturing company that shows consistent revenue growth above industry averages over the last three quarters, while operating cash flow remains stagnant. When analyzing accounts receivable, there is a disproportionate increase in sales to recently established related party customers. Which combination of analytical procedures best detects this potential fraud scheme?
questions.tag: Analytical Procedures | Related Party Transactions | Revenue Recognition
options: A. Apply Benford's Law to the first digits of invoices and calculate the accounts receivable turnover ratio compared to prior periods
B. Perform positive confirmation with all customers and manually review each transaction exceeding R$ 50,000
C. Implement segregation of duties in the sales department and require dual signatures on contracts
D. Analyze only electronic tax invoices and compare them against related party customer CNPJs in the Federal Revenue database
questions.justification_correct: Alternative A combines two key analytical procedures: (1) Benford's Law detects statistical anomalies in digit patterns suggesting manipulation or fraud in revenue recognition, and (2) receivables turnover analysis reveals whether new sales correspond to actual cash collections, exposing fictitious transactions with related parties.
questions.justification_wrong: B is a manual preventive/detective control, not an analytical procedure; C is a future preventive structural measure; D is limited documentary verification and does not address analytical procedures for detecting fraud schemes.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating transactions between a manufacturing company and its suppliers. During analysis, the examiner identified that purchase amounts exhibit an abnormal cyclical pattern: significant spikes at the end of each quarter, followed by partial returns at the beginning of the following quarter. Invoice values, when decomposed by first digit, consistently deviate from Benford's Law distribution. Which fraud scheme best explains these indicators?
questions.tag: Financial Statements Fraud - Revenue Recognition & Inventory Manipulation
options: A. Channel stuffing combined with improper revenue recognition, designed to inflate quarterly sales and smooth results using cookie jar reserves
B. Lapping of accounts receivable, where fictitious returns mask actual cash flow insufficiency
C. Inventory fraud through manipulation of physical counts to justify fluctuations in cost of goods sold
D. Related party transactions structured as legitimate sales to facilitate money laundering through the placement phase
questions.justification_correct: The described pattern—quarterly sales spikes followed by returns, Benford's Law deviations indicating data manipulation, and the artificial cyclical structure—is characteristic of channel stuffing: inflating period-end sales with products that customers return or that lack economic substance, combined with cookie jar accounting to smooth results.
questions.justification_wrong: Lapping (option B) involves specific accounts receivable schemes and concealment of bad debts, which would not justify the quarterly spike-and-return pattern. Inventory manipulation (C) would show corresponding COGS variations not apparent here.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a manufacturing company that reported anomalous sales growth over the past two quarters, concentrated with a single customer. Upon analyzing related party transactions, the examiner discovers this customer is an entity indirectly controlled by the company's CFO. Trend analysis reveals accounts receivable grew 45% while the cost of goods sold remained stable. Which indicator best indicates potential fraud?
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Related Party Transactions & Revenue Recognition
options: A. Sales growth exceeding 40% quarterly, which automatically violates Benford's Law for first digit distribution
B. The disproportionate relationship between accounts receivable growth (45%) and cost of goods sold stability, combined with a controlled related party transaction
C. The concentration of sales with a single customer, which by itself constitutes presumed fraud under American court jurisprudence
D. The CFO's indirect participation in the transaction, which automatically establishes management override and eliminates the need for additional risk analysis
questions.justification_correct: According to the CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024, the critical indicator is the accounting incongruity between revenue growth and cost of goods sold (accounting asymmetry) combined with controlled related party transactions. This mismatch suggests fictitious or side-agreement based revenue rather than legitimate sales.
questions.justification_wrong: A) Benford's Law does not establish percentage growth limits; accelerated growth is suspicious but does not automatically violate the Law. C) Customer concentration alone is not presumed fraud without additional indicators. D) Related party involvement requires substantive fraud risk analysis; it does not automatically establish override.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is analyzing transactions of a multinational company and identifies abnormal patterns in operations with overseas subsidiaries, including: (1) invoices from related party suppliers lacking adequate supporting documentation; (2) international transfers not corresponding to delivered goods or services; (3) suspense accounts remaining open exceeding 180 days; (4) significant variance between transaction frequency and supporting business contracts. Which set of analytical procedures most effectively detects potential fraud?
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Related Party Transactions, Money Laundering, and Analytical Procedures
options: A. Apply Benford's Law to first digits of invoice amounts, reconcile suspense accounts against original documentation, analyze transaction frequency patterns, and examine invoice approval authorization trails
B. Review only the final balance of accounts payable, compare gross margin percentages to prior period, and request balance confirmations from suppliers
C. Examine total transaction volume for the period, calculate operating profitability ratios, and document transaction approval policies
D. Analyze revenue growth trends, apply sensitivity testing to financial projections, and evaluate the overall control environment
questions.justification_correct: Alternative A represents the methodologically correct sequence for detecting fraud involving related parties and money laundering. Benford's Law is a specific tool for identifying invoice manipulation; suspense account reconciliation reveals unresolved transactions; frequency analysis detects structuring patterns typical of placement-phase money laundering; authorization review establishes management override risks.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative B fails because: (1) analyzing only the final balance ignores individual transactions where fraud resides; (2) gross margin comparison is ineffective for detecting transfer pricing schemes. Alternatives C and D focus on entity-level metrics rather than transaction-level fraud detection procedures.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating irregularities at a multinational manufacturing company. Analyzing financial statements over five years, the examiner identified that the accounts receivable turnover ratio showed consistent annual decline of 12%, while revenue growth remained stable at 8% annually. Simultaneously, the allowance for doubtful accounts remained constant at 2% of gross receivables despite the aging pattern deterioration. Which fraud scheme and detection procedure best fit these indicators?
questions.tag: Financial Statements Fraud - Revenue Recognition & Receivables Manipulation
options: A. Improper revenue recognition with undisclosed side agreements; most effective procedure: trend analysis of key ratios combined with detailed review of side agreements and cash collection analysis post-quarter end
B. Inventory manipulation through disguised channel stuffing; most effective procedure: Benford's Law application to the last digits of invoice amounts and physical inventory observation

C. Lapping of receipts with subsequent improper capitalization; most effective procedure: horizontal comparison of aging schedules of accounts receivable over consecutive periods with sample testing of subsequent cash receipts
D. Fictitious employee sales scheme; most effective procedure: test of details of sales transactions using statistical sampling methodology and reconciliation to commission records
questions.justification_correct: Alternative A best explains the observed indicators: declining receivables turnover combined with stable revenue indicates new sales are being recorded without corresponding cash collection, hallmark of fictitious or side-agreement-based revenue. The stable allowance despite aging deterioration suggests inadequate bad debt provisioning. Key detection: trend analysis reveals the ratio deterioration, side agreement review uncovers undisclosed terms, and post-period cash collection testing identifies which receivables never convert to cash.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative B: Channel stuffing typically involves inventory accumulation, not just receivables deceleration. Alternative C: Lapping would show receivables reduction, not stable allowance percentage. Alternative D: Fictitious employee sales would create isolated transactions, not systematic ratio deterioration across five years.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a multinational manufacturing company and identifies that the 'Advances to Suppliers' account presents a growing balance over the last 12 months, disproportionately elevated in relation to operational history and purchase volume. When applying analytical procedures, the examiner notes that advances do not correspond to traceable open purchase orders.
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Vendor Fraud & Analytical Procedures
options: A. Shell vendor scheme; apply Benford's Law analysis to advance payment values to detect systematic numerical manipulation
B. Improper revenue recognition; recalculate accounts payable turnover ratio and compare with prior periods
C. Ghost employee fraud combined with shell vendor scheme; trace reverse cash flows and verify bank account ownership
D. Improper capitalization; apply substantive tests on intangible asset composition recorded in the period
questions.justification_correct: Answer C is correct because it correctly identifies the most probable fraud: a conspirator employee, in collusion with owners or operators of fictitious suppliers, creates persistent advances with no corresponding operational substance.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative A is partially correct (shell vendor scheme is the fraud), but Benford's Law is an appropriate tool for large transaction volumes rather than the specific characteristics described.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating transactions of a multinational company and identifies that accounts receivable balances show anomalous growth of 45% in one quarter, while sales grew only 12% in the same period. When applying Benford's Law to the first digits of sales transaction values, the examiner observes significant deviations from the expected distribution.
questions.tag: Financial Data Analysis & Advanced Analytical Procedures
options: A. Improper revenue recognition with subsequent seasonality analysis and confirmation tests with customers to validate transaction reality
B. Channel stuffing combined with linear regression analysis to identify correlations between production volumes and customer indebtedness
C. Accounts receivable lapping with immediate bank reconciliation to detect deposits into third-party accounts
D. Financial statement fraud through cookie jar reserves with liquidity ratio analysis to evaluate overall solvency
questions.justification_correct: The scenario describes classic signals of improper revenue recognition: disproportionate accounts receivable growth relative to sales (primary red flag) and Benford's Law deviations (indicator of numerical manipulation).
questions.justification_wrong: B is incorrect because while channel stuffing would generate the observed pattern, linear regression analysis between production and indebtedness is not the appropriate investigative procedure.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is analyzing financial statements of a manufacturing company and identifies that accounts receivable increased 45% while sales increased only 12% in the same period. When applying Benford's Law to the last three digits of sales invoices, the examiner detects a distribution significantly deviating from the expected pattern. Which financial statement fraud scheme best explains these findings?
questions.tag: Analytical Procedures & Benford's Law Application - Revenue Recognition Fraud
options: A. Channel stuffing, where the company pressures distributors to purchase excessive inventory volumes, registering sales prematurely before actual customer demand
B. Cookie jar reserves, where the company creates excessive provisions in high-profitability periods to smooth results in future periods through reversals
C. Improper revenue recognition through fictitious invoices to shell customers, or duplicate invoices with altered values systematically recorded in the ledger
D. Inventory manipulation through circular transactions with related suppliers, inflating both revenues and assets simultaneously without actual cash flow
questions.justification_correct: The combination of disproportionate accounts receivable growth versus sales with significant Benford's Law deviation is highly indicative of improper revenue recognition through fictitious transactions. The deviation suggests systematic numerical manipulation.
questions.justification_wrong: A) Channel stuffing typically generates real sales (though premature) with approximate correspondence in sales volumes, not causing such severe Benford deviations; B) Cookie jar reserves affect provisions, not primary revenue transactions; D) Circular inventory transactions would show proportionate growth patterns.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a multinational manufacturing company that presents anomalous sales growth to related distributor entities overseas, accompanied by aged accounts receivable and subsequent product returns. When applying Benford's Law to the initial digits of these transaction values, the examiner observes significant deviation from the expected distribution.
questions.tag: Channel Stuffing + Related Party Transactions + Benford's Law
options: A. Channel stuffing associated with improper revenue recognition; the most effective preventive control would be segregation of duties between approval of sales and credit authorization
B. Inventory manipulation with premature revenue recognition; the preventive control would be only quarterly internal audit of accounts receivable balances
C. Revenue lapping combined with kiting; the appropriate preventive control would be random review of banking transactions without analytical pattern correlation
D. Shell vendor scheme disguised as legitimate sales; the preventive control would be relying on historical customer compliance without periodic creditworthiness review
questions.justification_correct: Answer A correctly identifies channel stuffing (artificial inflation of sales to related distributors) associated with improper revenue recognition, explaining the Benford's Law deviation through manipulation of transaction values.
questions.justification_wrong: Option B fails to recognize channel stuffing and proposes insufficient control (quarterly audit only). Option C confuses different fraud schemes (lapping and kiting are distinct). Option D suggests inadequate controls.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating a multinational company that presents anomalous sales growth to customers located in high-risk jurisdictions for money laundering. When applying Benford's Law to transaction values, the examiner observes significant deviations in the first decimal place, particularly abnormal concentration of digits '1' and '2'. Simultaneously, the examiner identifies transaction structuring just below mandatory reporting thresholds.
questions.tag: Money Laundering + Analytical Procedures + Structuring
options: A. Revenue recognition fraud with accounts receivable manipulation; implement continuous audit monitoring transaction aggregations by customer
B. Shell vendor scheme combined with illicit funds placement; review only formalized contracts and increase approval limits for transactions
C. Money laundering integration disguised as legitimate sales, with structuring to circumvent reporting limits; implement transaction monitoring system with automated threshold alerts
D. Lapping associated with financial statement fraud; apply Benford's Law monthly only to cash accounts without correlating patterns to customer risk profiles
questions.justification_correct: Answer C correctly identifies the fraud as integration (final stage of money laundering), which camouflages illicit funds as legitimate operational revenues. The Benford deviation combined with structuring just below reporting thresholds indicates deliberate manipulation.
questions.justification_wrong: A) Revenue recognition fraud does not explain structuring at reporting thresholds; B) Shell vendors and approval limits do not address the money laundering indicators; D) Cash-only analysis ignores the sales revenue component and structural patterns.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating transactions of a multinational company with suppliers in high-risk jurisdictions. During the analysis, the examiner identifies that the company uses multiple intermediate shell vendors before reaching the final supplier, with amounts fragmented into small transactions (below approval limits). Which fraud concealment technique is being employed?
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Shell Vendor Schemes & Structuring
options: A. Lapping of accounts payable; objective to conceal cash diversion through circular accounting records
B. Structuring (smurfing) associated with shell vendor scheme; objective to circumvent approval controls and conceal flows for money laundering
C. Transactional channel stuffing; objective to artificially inflate sales volume and deceive external auditors
D. Check alteration associated with related party transactions; objective to record fictitious expenses for cookie jar reserves
questions.justification_correct: Answer B is correct per CFE Manual 2024. Structuring (smurfing) deliberately fragments large transactions into smaller amounts to avoid detection and circumvent control thresholds.
questions.justification_wrong: A) Lapping involves time delay between records from different customers, not horizontal transaction fragmentation. C) Channel stuffing affects revenue recognition with real existing customers, not fictitious ones.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating transactions of a multinational company and identifies that a foreign subsidiary conducted multiple international transfers of small amounts (below USD 10,000) to various bank accounts in high-risk jurisdictions, systematically avoiding mandatory reporting limits. Which money laundering technique is being employed?
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Structuring and Transaction Pattern Analysis
options: A. Integration; analysis of international transaction networks with mapping of ultimate beneficial owners
B. Placement; reconciliation of bank balances with accounting records
C. Structuring (smurfing); analysis of transaction trends with identification of abnormal fragmentation patterns
D. Layering; completeness testing of supporting documentation for transfers
questions.justification_correct: Structuring (smurfing) is characterized by deliberate transaction fragmentation to avoid reporting limits (USD 10,000 threshold in the USA). The most effective analytical procedure is transaction trend analysis with identification of abnormal fragmentation patterns.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative A (Integration): although network analysis is useful, integration is the FINAL stage of resource recycling, not the identified phase. Alternative B addresses placement but focuses on reconciliation rather than pattern detection.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating transactions of a technology company that presents anomalous revenue growth in recent quarters. During analysis of accounts receivable, the examiner identifies that 35% of invoices were issued to entities established less than 60 days ago, located in low-tax jurisdictions, with no prior record of business operations with the company. Which fraud scheme is most likely?
questions.tag: Financial Statements Fraud - Revenue Recognition & Related Party Transactions
options: A. Channel stuffing fraud with fictitious customers; proceed with direct confirmation of accounts receivable from debtors and analyze cash collection flows
B. Fraudulent revenue recognition through shell vendors; perform substantive tests of transaction economic substance and trace cash flows back to source
C. Revenue skimming with collusion of salespeople; apply statistical sampling tests on invoices prior to 12 months
D. Kiting between customer accounts; execute regressive audit procedures on issued electronic invoices
questions.justification_correct: Alternative B correctly identifies the shell vendor scheme—a sophisticated variant of financial statement fraud where newly established entities (shell companies) in low-tax jurisdictions are used to recognize fictitious revenues without genuine economic substance.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative A (channel stuffing) is inadequate because this scheme involves pressuring real existing customers to purchase excessive volumes, not creating fictitious entities. Alternative C focuses on employee collusion rather than systematic shell entity creation.

questions.text: A fraud examiner is investigating suspicious transactions at a technology company presenting anomalous revenue growth in recent quarters. Upon applying Benford's Law to the first three digits of billing amounts, the examiner identifies a statistically significant deviation, with frequency much higher than expected for digits starting with '9'. What is the implication?
questions.tag: Financial Transactions & Fraud Schemes - Benford's Law and Forensic Analytical Analysis
options: A. Indicates possible channel stuffing or fraudulent revenue recognition; next step is to examine related party transactions and analyze transaction origins
B. Represents normal distribution expected in real financial data; no additional action is necessary
C. Suggests intentional value manipulation to maintain margins; should immediately investigate accounting entry procedures and authorization controls
D. Demonstrates financial statement fraud orchestrated by senior management; recommends applying Benford's Law to all asset line items
questions.justification_correct: Per CFE Fraud Examiners Manual 2024, deviations in Benford's Law applied to financial data indicate possible manipulation; excess of values starting with '9' is characteristic of frauds such as channel stuffing or fictitious revenue schemes designed to create round-number appearances.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative B ignores statistical evidence; Alternative C presumes a specific fraud scheme without progressive analytical validation; Alternative D proposes action scope that is premature without further investigation.

questions.text: During investigation of financial statement fraud at a multinational technology company, a CFE examiner identified that accounting reserves (cookie jar reserves) were systematically used to smooth results between quarters. The company created excessive provisions during high-profitability periods and reversed them during revenue decline periods, maintaining an artificial earnings pattern. Which procedure is MOST effective for detecting this scheme?
questions.tag: Trend and Ratio Analysis | Cookie Jar Reserves | Financial Statement Fraud
options: A. Vertical analysis of financial statements comparing the percentage of provisions relative to total assets between consecutive periods
B. Application of Benford's Law to the initial digits of provision account balances and verification of statistically significant deviations
C. Horizontal analysis and trend analysis of provision balances combined with testing of reversal of prior balances versus current accounting movement
D. Audit of related party transactions that may have originated the questioned provisions
questions.justification_correct: Alternative C is MOST effective because: (1) horizontal analysis detects abnormal variations in provision balances over time; (2) trend analysis reveals the artificial earnings smoothing pattern; (3) testing prior period reversals against current period movements directly identifies the cookie jar manipulation scheme.
questions.justification_wrong: Alternative A: although useful, vertical analysis alone does not detect temporal reversal of provisions. Alternative B: Benford's Law is more appropriate for transaction-level data than aggregate balance accounts. Alternative D: related party transaction audit may not identify internal reserve manipulation schemes.
